impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of the patent holder's unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, even when the tied products allegedly lack substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act for conditioning licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' agreements to exclusively purchase Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the antitrust damages award, finding that the question of whether the tied products had substantial non-infringing uses was properly submitted to the jury.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who bundle patent licenses with exclusive purchasing requirements for unpatented goods face significant antitrust liability risk. Companies must carefully structure licensing practices to avoid tying arrangements that foreclose competitors from adjacent product markets.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in patent prosecution and on a Walker Process fraud claim for attempted monopolization based on a fraudulently obtained patent.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgments on both inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud claims, finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. The case involves allegations that Tenaris committed inequitable conduct in obtaining coiled tubing patents by withholding prior art (the CYMAX Documents) from the PTO, and Global Tubing's counterclaim that this constituted attempted monopolization.
impacts.positiveImplications: Defendants accused of infringement have a viable path to challenge patents procured through inequitable conduct and to pursue Walker Process antitrust counterclaims when material facts are disputed.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face increased litigation risk where prior art known to the applicant was not disclosed during prosecution, as both inequitable conduct defenses and Walker Process fraud claims may survive summary judgment.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming methods and systems for tailoring content delivery specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's patents claiming server-based tailoring of content specifications for wireless device capabilities are ineligible for patenting under § 101, reversing the jury's infringement verdict and entering judgment for Netflix. The court found the claims directed to an abstract idea, requiring reversal of the $2.5 million damages award.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies defending against patent infringement claims involving content delivery and device optimization have a strengthened § 101 defense, potentially reducing exposure to patent assertion entities.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the content delivery and streaming technology space face heightened risk that their patents will be invalidated as abstract ideas under § 101, even after surviving lower court challenges and winning jury verdicts.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing discretionary denials of inter partes review institution constitute 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions guiding the Board's exercise of discretionary authority to decline IPR institution are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b). The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution and that the Director retains ultimate authority over non-institution decisions.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's ability to flexibly manage IPR institution decisions without being constrained by formal rulemaking procedures, preserving discretionary denials that may protect patent rights.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies seeking to challenge patents through IPR face continued uncertainty as the PTO Director can issue and modify institution guidance without formal rulemaking, potentially limiting access to the IPR process without meaningful public input.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations is consistent with 19 U.S.C. § 1516a and the common law right of access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that the ITC's blanket practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with the governing statute, and that 19 U.S.C. § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial records. The court affirmed the Court of International Trade's order directing the ITC to comply with statutory confidentiality requirements.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers and domestic industries participating in trade proceedings may benefit from greater transparency in judicial review of ITC determinations, potentially gaining access to information previously shielded by blanket confidentiality designations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that submit sensitive business information in ITC questionnaire responses face increased risk of disclosure, as the ITC can no longer automatically treat all responses as confidential without statutory justification.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether OPM's regulatory writing requirement for overtime authorization in 5 C.F.R. § 550.111(c) is a valid exercise of rulemaking authority under the statutory requirement in 5 U.S.C. § 5542(a) that the government pay for overtime 'officially ordered or approved.'
impacts.description: In an en banc decision, the Federal Circuit held that OPM's 80-year-old writing requirement for overtime authorization is a valid exercise of delegated rulemaking authority, affirming dismissal of Count II of a class action by IHS nurses who claimed they performed uncompensated overtime during the COVID-19 pandemic. However, the remaining counts alleging denial of nighttime, Sunday, and holiday pay enhancements were reversed and remanded.
impacts.positiveImplications: Federal agencies benefit from the validation of the overtime writing requirement, which provides a clear administrative framework for managing overtime liability and controlling costs.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal employees who perform overtime work without prior written authorization may be unable to recover overtime pay regardless of whether supervisors were aware of the work, potentially leaving significant uncompensated labor during emergencies.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether IHS nurses hired under Title 5 but paid under Title 38's special salary provisions are entitled to Title 38 enhanced compensation rates for overtime, nighttime, Sunday, and holiday work.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit panel reversed the Court of Federal Claims' dismissal of claims by IHS nurses seeking enhanced pay for nighttime, Sunday, holiday, and miscellaneous overtime work under 38 U.S.C. § 7453. The court found the nurses adequately stated claims that IHS was required to apply Title 38 compensation enhancements that were available to eligible Title 5 healthcare employees.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal healthcare agencies may face significant backpay liability for failing to apply Title 38 enhanced compensation provisions to eligible Title 5 healthcare employees, with potential class-wide implications for nurses across IHS facilities.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Restaurant Revitalization Fund statute creates a money-mandating obligation sufficient to support Tucker Act jurisdiction for restaurants that applied for but did not receive RRF grants.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that over 300 restaurants stated viable claims against the United States for unpaid RRF grants, holding that the statutory language directing the SBA to 'award grants to eligible entities in the order in which applications are received' created a money-mandating obligation enforceable under the Tucker Act. The SBA had processed applications out of order, paying later applicants before earlier ones.
impacts.positiveImplications: Small businesses denied government grants due to agency processing errors have a viable pathway to seek damages in the Court of Federal Claims when the underlying statute contains mandatory grant-disbursement language.
impacts.negativeImplications: The federal government faces potential liability for grants that were not awarded in statutory priority order, with implications for administration of future appropriated grant programs.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Board correctly construed claim terms in a patent directed to streaming partly encrypted media content, specifically regarding the scope of 'encryption information' in adaptive bitrate streaming systems.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the Board's claim construction in an IPR involving DivX's patent on streaming encrypted media content, vacated the Board's decision upholding the patent claims, and remanded for further proceedings. The ruling has implications for how encryption-related limitations in streaming technology patents are interpreted.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming technology companies benefit from the Federal Circuit's narrower construction of encryption-related claim terms, potentially invalidating patents that could otherwise be asserted against common adaptive bitrate streaming implementations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the digital content protection and streaming space may find their claims more vulnerable to invalidity challenges under the corrected claim construction.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment of no remedy based on that exclusion.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report in a patent infringement case against Microsoft's Azure platform, vacating the summary judgment that had been entered based on the absence of a damages remedy. The court found the district court applied an overly stringent standard to the expert's methodology.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs benefit from a more flexible standard for admissibility of damages expert opinions, reducing the risk that meritorious infringement claims are dismissed solely due to Daubert exclusions of damages evidence.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies defending patent suits may face broader exposure to damages claims as courts apply a less restrictive standard to patent damages expert testimony.

impacts.caseName: Game Plan, Inc. v. Uninterrupted IP, LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a party that acquires common law trademark rights through an asset purchase agreement executed after an opposition proceeding is filed can use those acquired rights to establish priority and cancel the opposer's registration.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the TTAB's cancellation of Game Plan's trademark registration for 'I AM MORE THAN AN ATHLETE' and dismissal of Game Plan's opposition to Uninterrupted IP's marks. UNIP successfully established priority through common law rights acquired via a 2019 asset purchase agreement, even though the acquisition occurred after the opposition was filed.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies can strategically acquire common law trademark rights through asset purchases to establish priority in TTAB proceedings, even after opposition proceedings have commenced.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trademark registrants face the risk that opponents can acquire prior common law rights mid-litigation to establish priority and cancel existing registrations, creating uncertainty in trademark enforcement.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming web conferencing systems with time-shifting capabilities for asynchronous viewing of multimedia data streams are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed dismissal of a patent infringement claim against Google, holding that the asserted web conferencing patent claims were ineligible under § 101. The claims, which covered systems enabling participants to view recorded portions of a web conference while the session was still live, were found to be directed to abstract ideas.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies defending against patent assertions involving web conferencing and multimedia streaming technologies have a strengthened § 101 defense at the pleading stage.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims directed to specific implementations of web conferencing features face increased vulnerability to early-stage dismissal under § 101.

impacts.caseName: Willis Electric Co., Ltd. v. Polygroup Ltd. (Macao Commercial Offshore)
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether substantial evidence supported the jury's finding that the claimed pre-lit artificial tree patent was not obvious and whether the damages award was supported.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the jury verdict finding Polygroup liable for infringing Willis Electric's patent on pre-lit artificial trees with integrated mechanical and electrical trunk connections, upholding the denial of JMOL on obviousness and sustaining the damages award. The court found substantial evidence supporting non-obviousness.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in consumer products benefit from the court's deference to jury findings on non-obviousness, particularly where commercial success and industry praise support the validity of the patent.
impacts.negativeImplications: Competitors face continued enforcement risk where jury verdicts on patent validity are supported by substantial evidence, limiting post-trial challenges to infringement and damages awards.

impacts.caseName: Crocs, Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found no violation of Section 337 based on failure to prove likelihood of confusion or dilution of Crocs's 3D trade dress marks, and whether the ITC properly issued only a limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed Crocs's untimely appeal of the ITC's no-violation finding as to active respondents and affirmed the ITC's entry of only a limited (rather than general) exclusion order against defaulting respondents for footwear infringing Crocs's 3D marks. The decision limits Crocs's ability to obtain broad import bans on competing casual footwear.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers of competing footwear products benefit from the narrow scope of the exclusion order, which applies only to defaulting respondents rather than all imports of similar products.
impacts.negativeImplications: Brand owners seeking broad import protection through ITC Section 337 actions face procedural hurdles on appeal timing and may obtain only limited exclusion orders even where some respondents default.

impacts.caseName: In re Bayou Grande Coffee Roasting Co.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the TTAB's refusal to register the mark KAHWA for cafés and coffee shops, based on the doctrine of foreign equivalents and the Kashmiri green tea meaning of the term, was supported by substantial evidence and constituted a new ground of refusal.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the TTAB's refusal to register the mark KAHWA for cafés and coffee shops. The court addressed the application of the doctrine of foreign equivalents and the evidentiary basis for finding the mark generic or merely descriptive based on an alternative foreign-language meaning as Kashmiri green tea.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses seeking to register foreign-language marks benefit from heightened scrutiny of the TTAB's application of the doctrine of foreign equivalents, particularly where the mark has multiple meanings across different languages and the asserted meaning is esoteric.
